method3.generated_article:
==Geography and ecology==
Jarvis Island is a small, uninhabited coral island located in the central equatorial Pacific Ocean, approximately 40 kilometers (25 miles) south of the equator at 0°22′S 160°01′W.[2] It is part of the central Line Islands, which are also known as the Teraina Islands.[2] The island has an emergent land area of 1,273 acres, with a mostly sandy landscape that reaches a maximum elevation of 7 meters (23 feet) at its highest point.[2][3] Geologically, Jarvis is the crest of an ancient coral reef cap situated on top of a massive underlying extinct volcano.[2] The center of the island consists of a dried former lagoon where deep deposits of guano have accumulated.[2] 

The island experiences a tropical desert climate characterized by high daytime temperatures, intense equatorial sun throughout the year, and relatively steady easterly trade winds.[2] Jarvis lacks a known natural freshwater lens and receives scant rainfall of less than 1 meter (40 inches) annually.[2] Because of these arid conditions, there are no plants larger than shrubs on the island.[2] The sparse vegetation consists of bunch grass, vines, and low-growing shrubs, which provide essential sheltering, roosting, and nesting habitats for shorebirds, land hermit crabs, and seabirds.[2] 

Jarvis Island hosts more nesting seabirds than any other island in the United States tropical Pacific Islands, supporting 14 species of breeding seabirds.[2][3] The island is home to an estimated one million sooty terns and is the only protected seabird island in the northern Line Islands.[1][3] Historically, the island's environment suffered large impacts from guano mining as well as the introduction of invasive cats and rats.[1] Following the eradication of these invasive species, seabird populations have recovered, including the return of shearwaters to the island.[1]

The marine ecosystem surrounding Jarvis Island is exceptionally productive due to wind-driven equatorial upwelling and topographic upwelling.[2] The shallow marine benthic habitats feature a fringing reef with shallow back reefs, a steep forereef, and a moderate-sized reef terrace on the eastern windward side.[2] Because of its remoteness and a lack of human impacts, this fringing reef supports a healthy coral community and a diverse array of native marine organisms.[3] The submerged lands and waters within the island's three-mile territorial limit encompass an additional 36,214 acres.[3]

==History==
The first known sighting of Jarvis Island by Europeans occurred on August 21, 1821, by Captain Brown aboard the British ship *Eliza Francis*.[2][4][5] The island was named in honor of Edward, Thomas, and William Jarvis, the owners of the vessel.[2] After American seafarers discovered guano deposits on the island, the United States Exploring Expedition was sent to survey the area in 1840.[2] The United States subsequently claimed the island under the Guano Islands Act of 1856.[2][4][5] In March 1857, representatives Benson and Charles H. Judd took formal possession of Jarvis Island on behalf of the American Guano Company of New York.[2][4] Commander Charles Henry Davis of the USS *St. Mary* further surveyed the island later that year and officially claimed it for the United States on February 27, 1858.[1][4] Guano mining operations began in 1857 and continued until the United States abandoned the island in 1879, by which time approximately 8,000 tonnes of guano had been removed.[1][4][5] 

The United Kingdom formally annexed the island in June 1889, but did not exploit its resources or develop the territory.[1][4][5] Although the island was leased to the Pacific Phosphate Company of London in 1906, mining operations were never resumed.[4] The United States reclaimed and reoccupied Jarvis Island as an unincorporated territory in March 1935.[1][4][5] On May 13, 1936, President Franklin D. Roosevelt signed Executive Order 7368, which placed the island under the jurisdiction of the Secretary of the Interior.[3] During World War II, Japanese forces attacked the island in 1942, which forced the evacuation of the American colonists.[1] 

On June 27, 1974, the Secretary of the Interior designated the island and its territorial waters out to a 3-nautical-mile limit as a unit of the National Wildlife Refuge System.[3] The refuge is currently administered by the United States Fish and Wildlife Service.[4] In 2009, Jarvis Island was additionally designated as part of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[5]

==Transportation==
The only method of transportation to Jarvis Island is by marine vessels capable of undertaking extended open-ocean voyages.[3] The island is located approximately 1,263 nautical miles from Honolulu, Hawaii, and a ship journey to reach the remote location takes an average of eight days.[3] In recent years, transportation has been provided by the U.S. Coast Guard, private charter vessels, and the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA).[3] NOAA has utilized ships such as the *Townsend Cromwell* and the *Oscar Elton Sette* to conduct regular scientific cruises to the island.[5] The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service has also relied on chartered vessels for management actions and has considered utilizing an agency-owned vessel to provide transportation for field camps.[1][3]

Accessing the island presents significant operational limitations, including a lack of reliable and affordable transportation, extreme environmental conditions, and safety and logistical concerns associated with landing.[3] When transporting fuel to the island to power electrical generators, it must be carried in U.S. Department of Transportation-approved containers that have been tested for leaks and weigh less than 50 pounds (23 kilograms) each.[1]

==Military==
The 1935 reoccupation of the territory was driven by the American Equatorial Islands Colonization Project, which aimed to construct weather stations and landing fields for military and commercial use along air routes between California and Australia.[1] The U.S. government also utilized the colonization of Jarvis, Howland, and Baker Islands as a strategic check against the eastern territorial expansion of Japan.[1] The colonists who occupied the island, known as the Hui Panalāʻau, included over 130 young men from Hawaii, many of whom were Native Hawaiian students recruited from the Kamehameha Schools.[1][2] 

To facilitate small boat access for military and historical activities, short channels were blasted through the shallow fringing reefs.[4] Remnants of these channels remain evident today in the island's West and East Terrace georegions.[4] Although the island is now an uninhabited wildlife refuge, hazards associated with human habitation during the island's military era may still remain on land or wash ashore.[3]

==National Wildlife Refuge==
Jarvis Island National Wildlife Refuge is managed by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service (USFWS) and is located 1,305 nautical miles south of Honolulu.[2] The protected area encompasses 429,853 acres, which includes 1,273 acres of emergent terrestrial land and 428,580 acres of submerged lands extending up to 200 nautical miles from the island.[2] Originally, the refuge boundary only extended to the three-mile territorial sea limit, covering a total of 37,487 acres.[5] 

The primary mission of the refuge is to conserve, manage, and restore the island's fish, wildlife, and plant resources for the benefit of future generations.[2][5] Upon its transfer to the USFWS, management goals emphasized the preservation of the complete terrestrial and marine ecosystems, with a particular focus on protecting the island's significant seabird nesting colonies.[5] To guide the administration and management of the refuge, the USFWS developed a Comprehensive Conservation Plan in 2008.[4][5]

Access to the remote refuge is highly restricted and infrequent.[3] In August 2024, a USFWS biology team visited the island, marking the agency's first trip to Jarvis Island in over six years.[3] Occasionally, the USFWS evaluates limited public access for specific activities. For instance, the agency has considered allowing amateur radio operations on the island, provided such activities do not detract from the core mission of protecting the natural character of the refuge's terrestrial and marine resources.[2]
